back_translation: Later, after these events, when the wrath of King Ahasuerus had subsided, he remembered Queen Vashti, what she had done, and what had been decreed to punish her.
Then the king's attendants who served him said, 'Let beautiful young virgins be sought out for the king.

Let the king appoint commissioners in every province of the kingdom to gather all the beautiful young virgins to the harem at the citadel-city of Susa, under the care of Hegai the king's eunuch in charge of the women, and let beauty treatments be provided for them.
Let the young woman whom the king favors most become queen in place of Vashti.' This proposal pleased the king, and he had it carried out accordingly.
There was a Jewish man in the citadel-city of Susa named Mordecai son of Jair, son of Shimei, son of Kish, of the tribe of Benjamin.
He had been carried into exile from Jerusalem among the captives taken away with Jeconiah king of Judah, whom Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon had carried off.

Mordecai had raised Hadassah, that is Esther, his uncle's daughter, because she had no father or mother. This young woman was beautiful in form and appearance, and when her father and mother died, Mordecai took her as his own daughter.

Later, when the king's command and edict were proclaimed, and many young women were gathered to the citadel-city of Susa under Hegai's care, Esther too was taken to the palace under the care of Hegai the keeper of the women.

The young woman pleased him and obtained his favor, so he hurried to provide her beauty treatments and nourishing food, along with seven choice maidservants from the palace, and he moved her and her maids to the best place in the harem.
Esther did not tell anyone her people or her lineage, because Mordecai had charged her not to tell.
Every single day Mordecai would walk back and forth in front of the court of the harem to learn how Esther was and what was happening to her.

Before each young woman's turn came to go to King Ahasuerus, she had to complete twelve months of treatments according to the regulation for the women — six months with oil of myrrh and six months with perfumes and women's cosmetics.
When a young woman's turn came to go to the king, she was given whatever she asked to take with her from the harem to the palace.

She would go in the evening and return in the morning to a second harem under the care of Shaashgaz the king's eunuch in charge of the concubines. She would not go to the king again unless the king delighted in her and summoned her by name.

When Esther's turn came — the daughter of Abihail, Mordecai's uncle, who had taken her as his daughter — to go to the king, she asked for nothing except what Hegai the king's eunuch in charge of the women advised. And Esther was favored in the eyes of all who saw her.
So Esther was taken to King Ahasuerus at the royal residence in the tenth month, the month of Tebeth, in the seventh year of his reign.
The king loved Esther more than all the other women; she was favored and pleasing to him more than all the virgins. So he set the royal crown on her head and made her queen in place of Vashti.
Then the king held a great banquet for all his officials and servants — Esther's banquet. He proclaimed a tax holiday for the provinces and gave gifts with royal liberality.
When the virgins were gathered a second time, Mordecai was sitting at the king's gate.
Esther still had not revealed her lineage or her people, as Mordecai had charged her; she continued to obey Mordecai's command as when she was under his care.
In those days, while Mordecai was sitting at the king's gate, Bigthan and Teresh, two of the king's eunuchs who guarded the threshold, became angry and plotted to assassinate King Ahasuerus.
The matter became known to Mordecai, who reported it to Queen Esther, and Queen Esther told the king in Mordecai's name.
When the matter was investigated and found to be true, the two were hanged on the gallows, and this event was recorded in the book of the chronicles in the king's presence.
